The blood-brain barrier is formed by tight junctions between endothelial cells of cerebral capillaries.
Febrile seizures occur in 3-4% of children aged 6 months to 5 years and are the most common seizure type in childhood.
The cerebellum contains more neurons than the rest of the brain combined — approximately 69 billion.
Myelination begins in the peripheral nervous system at ~4 months gestation and continues into the third decade of life.
Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) is caused by mutations in the SMN1 gene on chromosome 5q13.
The hippocampus is named after the Greek word for 'seahorse' due to its curved shape.
Absence seizures can be provoked by hyperventilation in >80% of untreated patients.
Duchenne muscular dystrophy affects approximately 1 in 3,500-5,000 live male births worldwide.
The optic nerve is technically a CNS tract, not a peripheral nerve — it is myelinated by oligodendrocytes.
West syndrome (infantile spasms + hypsarrhythmia + developmental regression) was first described in 1841.
The vagus nerve (CN X) innervates structures from the brainstem all the way to the splenic flexure of the colon.
Tuberous sclerosis complex is caused by mutations in TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) genes.
The substantia nigra gets its dark color from neuromelanin, a byproduct of dopamine synthesis.
Guillain-Barre syndrome has an annual incidence of ~1-2 per 100,000 people worldwide.
The Circle of Willis is complete in only about 25-50% of the population.
Lennox-Gastaut syndrome accounts for 1-4% of childhood epilepsies and is characterized by multiple seizure types.
Phenytoin follows zero-order kinetics at therapeutic levels — small dose increases can cause toxicity.
The neonatal brain consumes approximately 60% of the body's total energy, compared to ~20% in adults.
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) is the most common single-gene disorder in neurology, affecting ~1 in 3,000.
The anterior fontanelle typically closes between 9-18 months of age.
Rett syndrome is caused by mutations in MECP2 and affects almost exclusively females.
The brainstem contains all cranial nerve nuclei except those of CN I (olfactory) and CN II (optic).
Dravet syndrome is most commonly caused by SCN1A mutations and presents with prolonged (hemiclonic) febrile seizures in the first year.
Levetiracetam works by binding to synaptic vesicle protein SV2A.
The lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus has 6 layers and relays visual information to the primary visual cortex.
Alexander disease is caused by mutations in GFAP and is characterized by Rosenthal fibers on pathology.
The EEG of a term newborn should show trace alternant pattern during quiet sleep.
Krabbe disease (globoid cell leukodystrophy) is caused by deficiency of galactosylceramidase.
The medial longitudinal fasciculus connects the abducens nucleus to the contralateral oculomotor nucleus for conjugate gaze.
Childhood absence epilepsy has a characteristic 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave pattern on EEG.
The watershed zones of the brain are most vulnerable to hypoxic-ischemic injury.
Sturge-Weber syndrome is caused by a somatic mosaic mutation in GNAQ.
The Babinski sign is normally present in infants up to ~12-24 months due to incomplete corticospinal tract myelination.
Myasthenia gravis is caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction.
The thalamus serves as the relay center for almost all sensory information except olfaction.
ACTH is the first-line treatment for infantile spasms of unknown etiology.
The internal capsule carries the corticospinal tract in its posterior limb.
Canavan disease is caused by aspartoacylase deficiency leading to accumulation of N-acetylaspartic acid.
The EEG shows burst suppression in severe neonatal encephalopathies such as Ohtahara syndrome.
Chiari II malformation is almost always associated with myelomeningocele.
Benign rolandic epilepsy (BECTS) is the most common idiopathic focal epilepsy of childhood.
The lenticulostriate arteries are branches of the MCA and supply the basal ganglia.
Metachromatic leukodystrophy is caused by arylsulfatase A deficiency.
The stretch reflex arc involves only two neurons — a sensory neuron and a motor neuron (monosynaptic).
Aicardi syndrome features infantile spasms, agenesis of the corpus callosum, and chorioretinal lacunae.
Ethosuximide works by blocking T-type calcium channels and is specific for absence seizures.
The posterior fossa is the most common location for brain tumors in children.
Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease is an X-linked disorder caused by mutations in PLP1.
The pyramidal decussation occurs at the junction of the medulla and spinal cord.
Landau-Kleffner syndrome presents with acquired epileptic aphasia and continuous spike-wave during sleep.
Mitochondrial disorders can affect any organ system but preferentially affect tissues with high energy demands.
The dorsal columns carry proprioception and fine touch, decussating in the medulla.
Zellweger syndrome is a peroxisomal biogenesis disorder presenting with severe hypotonia and seizures in neonates.
Wilson disease involves copper accumulation due to ATP7B mutations — Kayser-Fleischer rings are pathognomonic.
The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord.
Rasmussen encephalitis is a progressive unilateral brain inflammation that causes intractable focal seizures.
The clasp-knife phenomenon (initial resistance then sudden release) is characteristic of upper motor neuron spasticity.
Joubert syndrome is characterized by the 'molar tooth sign' on MRI due to cerebellar vermis hypoplasia.
Carbamazepine & oxcarbazepine can worsen absence and myoclonic seizures by enhancing thalamocortical synchronization.
The uncus is the most common structure to herniate in transtentorial herniation, compressing CN III.
Biotinidase deficiency is a treatable cause of seizures, hearing loss, and skin rash in infants.
The dorsal root ganglion contains the cell bodies of primary sensory neurons.
Panayiotopoulos syndrome presents with autonomic seizures (vomiting, pallor) and has an excellent prognosis.
Adrenoleukodystrophy is an X-linked peroxisomal disorder with elevated very-long-chain fatty acids.
The Romberg test assesses dorsal column function — positive when patients sway with eyes closed.
The arcuate fasciculus connects Broca's and Wernicke's areas — damage causes conduction aphasia.
Vigabatrin is preferred over ACTH for infantile spasms caused by tuberous sclerosis complex.
The cavernous sinus contains CN III, IV, V1, V2, and VI — and the internal carotid artery.
Menkes disease is an X-linked copper transport disorder caused by ATP7A mutations.
The EEG in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy shows 4-6 Hz generalized polyspike-and-wave discharges.
Holoprosencephaly results from incomplete division of the prosencephalon and is associated with midline facial defects.
Topiramate inhibits carbonic anhydrase, which can cause metabolic acidosis and kidney stones.
The superior cerebellar artery supplies the superior cerebellum and is the most common vessel in SCA stroke.
Maple syrup urine disease is caused by branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency.
The corneal reflex tests CN V1 (afferent) and CN VII (efferent).
Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy is caused by ALDH7A1 mutations and responds to vitamin B6.
The nucleus solitarius receives taste and visceral afferents from CN VII, IX, and X.
Lissencephaly (smooth brain) can result from LIS1 or DCX gene mutations.
Valproic acid is contraindicated in children under 2 on polytherapy due to hepatotoxicity risk.
The spinothalamic tract carries pain and temperature, decussating at the level of entry in the spinal cord.
Angelman syndrome is caused by loss of maternal UBE3A and features seizures, ataxia, and a happy demeanor.
The red nucleus receives input from the cerebellum and gives rise to the rubrospinal tract.
Periventricular leukomalacia is the most common brain injury in premature infants.
Clobazam is a 1,5-benzodiazepine with less sedation than 1,4-benzodiazepines like clonazepam.
The pineal gland produces melatonin and is one of the few midline structures — useful for identifying midline shift.
Leigh syndrome is the most common mitochondrial disease of childhood, affecting the basal ganglia and brainstem.
The cremasteric reflex is mediated by L1-L2 nerve roots.
GLUT1 deficiency syndrome causes seizures and developmental delay treatable with a ketogenic diet.
The choroid plexus produces approximately 500 mL of cerebrospinal fluid per day.
Photosensitive epilepsy is most commonly seen in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy and Dravet syndrome.
The edinger-westphal nucleus provides parasympathetic innervation to the pupil via CN III.
Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is caused by persistent measles virus infection.
The lateral corticospinal tract controls fine motor movements of the distal extremities.
Megalencephalic leukoencephalopathy with subcortical cysts (MLC) is caused by MLC1 mutations.
The H-reflex is the electrical equivalent of the ankle stretch reflex and tests the S1 nerve root.
Progressive myoclonic epilepsies include Unverricht-Lundborg, Lafora body disease, and neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses.
The trigeminal nerve (CN V) is the largest cranial nerve by diameter.
Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection causing sensorineural hearing loss.
The putamen and caudate nucleus together form the striatum — the primary input structure of the basal ganglia.
Lennox-Gastaut syndrome often evolves from West syndrome, a phenomenon known as epileptic encephalopathy evolution.
The muscle spindle detects muscle stretch, while the Golgi tendon organ detects muscle tension.
Alternating hemiplegia of childhood is most commonly caused by ATP1A3 mutations.
The fasciculus gracilis carries sensory information from the lower extremities (below T6).
Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the most common autoimmune encephalitis in children.
The area postrema is a circumventricular organ that lacks a blood-brain barrier — it mediates vomiting.
Neuronal migration occurs primarily between weeks 12-24 of gestation.
The longest nerve in the body is the sciatic nerve, running from the lumbosacral plexus to the foot.
Ohtahara syndrome (early infantile epileptic encephalopathy) presents in the first 3 months with tonic spasms.
The Purkinje cells of the cerebellum are among the largest neurons in the human brain.
Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is a monophasic demyelinating disease that typically follows infection.
The Weber test lateralizes to the affected ear in conductive hearing loss and to the normal ear in sensorineural loss.
Mucopolysaccharidoses are lysosomal storage disorders — Hunter syndrome (MPS II) is the only X-linked type.
The blood supply to the spinal cord is most tenuous in the mid-thoracic region (watershed zone).
Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus occurs only during sleep and stops when the infant is awakened.
The facial nerve (CN VII) has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve.
Glucose transporter type 1 deficiency should be suspected when CSF glucose is low with normal serum glucose.
The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) is critical for diaphragm function — 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.'
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCL/Batten disease) are the most common neurodegenerative disorders of childhood.
The posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) occlusion causes lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome.
Breath-holding spells occur in up to 5% of children aged 6 months to 6 years and are benign.
The Meyer loop carries inferior visual field fibers through the temporal lobe to the primary visual cortex.
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is the most common inborn error of amino acid metabolism.
The foramen of Monro connects the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle.
Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline.
The mylohyoid nerve is a motor branch of V3 (mandibular division of trigeminal) — not the hypoglossal nerve.
Pompe disease (glycogen storage disease type II) can present as a floppy infant with cardiomegaly.
The dentate nucleus is the largest deep cerebellar nucleus and is involved in planning voluntary movements.
Childhood stroke has an incidence of approximately 2-13 per 100,000 children per year.
The nucleus ambiguus in the medulla gives rise to motor fibers of CN IX, X, and XI.
Neonatal herpes encephalitis most commonly affects the temporal lobes.
The Charcot-Marie-Tooth diseases are the most common inherited peripheral neuropathies.
Pilocytic astrocytoma is the most common pediatric brain tumor and has an excellent prognosis.
The inferior olivary nucleus is the sole source of climbing fibers to the cerebellum.
Congenital myotonic dystrophy is caused by CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the DMPK gene.
The anterior choroidal artery supplies the posterior limb of the internal capsule, optic tract, and hippocampus.
Children with epilepsy have a 5-fold increased risk of ADHD compared to the general population.
The mesial temporal structures are the most epileptogenic region of the brain.
Acute cerebellar ataxia is the most common cause of acute ataxia in children aged 2-5 years.
The Onuf nucleus in the sacral spinal cord controls voluntary sphincter function.
Galactosemia can present with E. coli sepsis in neonates due to impaired immune function.
The corticobulbar tract provides bilateral innervation to most cranial nerve motor nuclei except the lower face.
PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections) remains controversial.
The zona incerta is a subthalamic structure increasingly recognized as a deep brain stimulation target.
Nonketotic hyperglycinemia presents in neonates with seizures, hiccups, and a burst-suppression EEG pattern.
The lateral lemniscus is the primary ascending auditory pathway in the brainstem.
Hemimegalencephaly is a malformation of cortical development causing enlargement of one cerebral hemisphere.
The hippocampal commissure (psalterium) connects the two hippocampi across the midline.
Todd's paralysis — transient postictal weakness — can last minutes to hours and mimics stroke.
The mammillary bodies are part of the Papez circuit and are affected early in Wernicke encephalopathy.
Focal cortical dysplasia is the most common cause of medically refractory epilepsy requiring surgery in children.
The superficial middle cerebral vein (vein of Labbe) drains the lateral surface of the temporal lobe.
Spinal muscular atrophy with respiratory distress (SMARD1) is caused by IGHMBP2 mutations.
The claustrum is the thinnest deep gray matter structure and its function remains largely mysterious.
Nusinersen (Spinraza) was the first FDA-approved treatment for SMA, delivered intrathecally.
The locus coeruleus is the principal site of norepinephrine synthesis in the brain.
Polymicrogyria results from abnormal late neuronal migration or early cortical organization.
The pedunculopontine nucleus is involved in gait initiation and REM sleep regulation.
Childhood absence epilepsy has a 70-80% remission rate by adolescence.
The septum pellucidum separates the anterior horns of the lateral ventricles.
Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome in children should prompt evaluation for neuroblastoma.
The middle cerebellar peduncle (brachium pontis) is the largest cerebellar peduncle.
Leukodystrophies affect white matter while poliodystrophies primarily affect gray matter.
The cuneate fasciculus carries sensory information from the upper extremities (above T6).
Ketogenic diet can be effective for GLUT1 deficiency, Dravet syndrome, and drug-resistant epilepsy.
The interpeduncular cistern (basal cistern) is a key landmark for identifying CN III emergence.
Spina bifida occulta is found incidentally in ~10-20% of the general population.
The extreme capsule connects the claustrum to the insular cortex.
Ragged red fibers on muscle biopsy with modified Gomori trichrome stain suggest mitochondrial myopathy.
The trochlear nerve (CN IV) is the only cranial nerve that exits dorsally and has the longest intracranial course.
Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy is caused by mutations in nicotinic acetylcholine receptor genes.
The fornix carries output from the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies and septal nuclei.
Cerebral palsy is the most common motor disability in childhood, affecting 2-3 per 1,000 live births.
The subcommissural organ secretes Reissner's fiber, which may play a role in CSF flow dynamics.
Herpes simplex encephalitis in neonates is most commonly caused by HSV-2, while in older children by HSV-1.
The alveus is the thin white matter layer covering the hippocampal surface.
Epilepsia partialis continua is focal motor status epilepticus that can last days to years, and when presents in young child with psychomotor delays should raise suspicion for POLG-relates Alpers.
Friedreich ataxia is caused by GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FXN gene, leading to frataxin deficiency.
The anterior cerebral artery supplies the medial surface of the frontal and parietal lobes — occlusion causes contralateral leg weakness.
Tay-Sachs disease is caused by hexosaminidase A deficiency and features a cherry-red spot on fundoscopy.
Congenital muscular dystrophies are distinguished from other muscular dystrophies by onset at birth or within the first 6 months.
The abducens nerve (CN VI) has the longest subarachnoid course and is most susceptible to increased intracranial pressure.
Sandhoff disease is clinically similar to Tay-Sachs but involves deficiency of both hexosaminidase A and B.
The corticospinal tract does not fully myelinate until approximately age 2 years.
Moyamoya disease is characterized by progressive stenosis of the distal internal carotid arteries with collateral 'puff of smoke' vessels.
Niemann-Pick type C is a lysosomal storage disorder presenting with vertical supranuclear gaze palsy and ataxia.
Congenital fiber type disproportion is a congenital myopathy with predominance of small type 1 fibers on biopsy.
The globus pallidus interna is the primary output nucleus of the basal ganglia.
Neuroblastoma is the most common extracranial solid tumor in children and can present with opsoclonus-myoclonus.
Benign familial neonatal seizures are caused by mutations in KCNQ2 or KCNQ3 potassium channel genes.
The posterior communicating artery is the most common site of aneurysm causing CN III palsy.
Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal disorder caused by alpha-galactosidase A deficiency, causing painful neuropathy.
The nucleus basalis of Meynert is the primary cholinergic nucleus of the forebrain and degenerates in Alzheimer disease.
Congenital myasthenic syndromes are genetic (not autoimmune) disorders of the neuromuscular junction.
The subthalamic nucleus is hyperactive in Parkinson disease — deep brain stimulation targets it to reduce motor symptoms.
Gaucher disease type 2 (acute neuropathic) presents in infancy with bulbar signs and rapid neurological decline.
The tectospinal tract originates from the superior colliculus and mediates reflexive head turning toward visual stimuli.
Hereditary spastic paraplegias are a group of genetic disorders characterized by progressive lower extremity spasticity.
The dorsal midbrain (Parinaud) syndrome features upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and light-near dissociation.
Central core disease is a congenital myopathy associated with RYR1 mutations and malignant hyperthermia susceptibility.
The anterior commissure connects the temporal lobes and is an important landmark in neuroimaging.
Ataxia-telangiectasia is caused by ATM gene mutations and features cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and immunodeficiency.
The ventral posterior lateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus relays somatosensory information from the body.
Lafora body disease is a progressive myoclonic epilepsy caused by mutations in EPM2A or NHLRC1.
The genu of the internal capsule carries corticobulbar fibers to the face and tongue motor nuclei.
Ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency is the most common urea cycle disorder and is X-linked.
Myotubular myopathy (X-linked centronuclear) is caused by MTM1 mutations and presents with severe neonatal hypotonia.
Sialidosis type I presents with cherry-red spot and myoclonus without dysmorphic features.
Infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy (INAD) is caused by PLA2G6 mutations and features cerebellar atrophy on MRI.
The external capsule lies between the putamen and the claustrum.
Nemaline myopathy is characterized by rod-shaped nemaline bodies on muscle biopsy and presents with hypotonia.
The medial lemniscus carries fine touch and proprioception from the dorsal column nuclei to the thalamus.
GM1 gangliosidosis is caused by beta-galactosidase deficiency and can present with a cherry-red spot.
Pelizaeus-Merzbacher-like disease (PMLD) is caused by GJC2 mutations and mimics PMD but with autosomal recessive inheritance.
The globus pallidus externa is part of the indirect pathway of the basal ganglia, facilitating movement inhibition.
Refsum disease is a peroxisomal disorder causing peripheral neuropathy, retinitis pigmentosa, and cerebellar ataxia.
Centronuclear myopathy is characterized by centrally placed nuclei on muscle biopsy rather than the normal peripheral position.
The vestibulospinal tract maintains balance and posture by facilitating extensor muscle tone.
Smith-Lemli-Opitz syndrome is caused by a defect in cholesterol biosynthesis (7-dehydrocholesterol reductase deficiency).
The medial geniculate nucleus of the thalamus relays auditory information to the primary auditory cortex.
The alvear pathway is an alternative route from the entorhinal cortex to the hippocampus, alongside the perforant path.
MECP2 duplication syndrome affects males and causes intellectual disability, recurrent infections, and progressive spasticity.
The ventral anterior (VA) nucleus of the thalamus receives basal ganglia output and projects to the premotor cortex.
Vanishing white matter disease is caused by mutations in EIF2B genes and worsens with febrile illness or minor head trauma.
Miller Fisher syndrome is a GBS variant featuring ataxia, areflexia, and ophthalmoplegia — associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies.
The nucleus accumbens is part of the ventral striatum and plays a central role in reward and motivation circuits.
Biotin-thiamine-responsive basal ganglia disease (SLC19A3) is a treatable cause of acute encephalopathy in children.
CDKL5 deficiency disorder features early-onset seizures, severe intellectual disability, and was previously classified under Rett syndrome.
The rubrospinal tract facilitates flexor muscle tone and is most prominent in the cervical spinal cord.
Propionic acidemia can present with metabolic stroke affecting the basal ganglia.
Agenesis of the corpus callosum occurs in approximately 1 in 4,000 births and can be isolated or syndromic.
The anterior perforated substance is pierced by the lenticulostriate arteries entering the basal ganglia.
Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) includes PKAN, caused by PANK2 mutations with 'eye of the tiger' sign on MRI.
Aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC) deficiency causes combined dopamine and serotonin deficiency with oculogyric crises.
Congenital disorders of glycosylation (CDG) often present with inverted nipples, abnormal fat pads, and cerebellar hypoplasia.
Adenylosuccinate lyase deficiency is a purine synthesis disorder causing seizures, autistic features, and psychomotor delay.
The spiral ganglion contains the cell bodies of auditory nerve fibers within the modiolus of the cochlea.
Schizencephaly is a malformation of cortical development featuring a cleft lined by gray matter extending from ventricle to surface.
The posterior limb of the internal capsule can be used on neonatal MRI to predict motor outcomes after HIE.
Methylmalonic acidemia can cause metabolic stroke of the basal ganglia, similar to propionic acidemia.
The mesencephalic nucleus of CN V is unique — it contains primary sensory neuron cell bodies within the CNS itself.
Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome (Ondine's curse) is caused by PHOX2B mutations affecting autonomic respiratory control.
Sulfite oxidase deficiency presents like severe HIE with lens subluxation in neonates.
The hippocampal formation includes the dentate gyrus, hippocampus proper (CA1-CA4), and subiculum.
Acute necrotizing encephalopathy of childhood is associated with bilateral thalamic necrosis, often triggered by influenza.
The suprachiasmatic nucleus of the hypothalamus is the master circadian pacemaker.
Septo-optic dysplasia (de Morsier syndrome) features optic nerve hypoplasia, pituitary dysfunction, and midline brain defects.
The posterior columns are organized somatotopically — sacral fibers are most medial, cervical fibers most lateral.
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency can cause neonatal jaundice severe enough to produce kernicterus.
Cerebral folate deficiency can cause seizures and developmental regression — treatable with folinic acid.
Neuroacanthocytosis features chorea, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, and acanthocytes on blood smear.
Molybdenum cofactor deficiency is clinically identical to sulfite oxidase deficiency with refractory neonatal seizures.
The dentatorubrothalamic tract connects the dentate nucleus through the superior cerebellar peduncle to the contralateral red nucleus and thalamus.
Segawa disease (dopa-responsive dystonia) is caused by GCH1 mutations and shows dramatic response to levodopa.
The lateral hypothalamus contains orexin/hypocretin neurons — their loss causes narcolepsy type 1.
Glutaric aciduria type 1 is caused by GCDH deficiency and presents with macrocephaly and acute striatal necrosis.
The spinocerebellar tracts carry proprioceptive information to the cerebellum — the dorsal tract enters via the inferior peduncle.
Congenital infection with toxoplasmosis classically produces diffuse intracranial calcifications, unlike CMV which causes periventricular calcifications.
The pretectal nucleus mediates the consensual pupillary light reflex by sending bilateral projections to the Edinger-Westphal nuclei.
Creatine deficiency syndromes (GAMT, AGAT, SLC6A8) are treatable causes of intellectual disability and seizures.
The flocculonodular lobe of the cerebellum (vestibulocerebellum) controls balance and eye movements.
Acute flaccid myelitis (AFM) is associated with enterovirus D68 and causes poliomyelitis-like limb weakness.
The dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus provides parasympathetic innervation to thoracic and abdominal viscera.
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia encompasses a group of prenatal-onset neurodegenerative disorders with cerebellar and pontine atrophy.
Urea cycle disorders present with hyperammonemia, and respiratory alkalosis (from central hyperventilation) is an early clue.
The tuber cinereum is the hypothalamic region between the optic chiasm and mammillary bodies — hamartomas here cause gelastic seizures.
Werdnig-Hoffmann disease (SMA type 1) was previously (prior to newborn screen and the availability of gene-based treatments) the most common genetic cause of infant death.
Febrile infection-related epilepsy syndrome (FIRES) may respond to the ketogenic diet when conventional therapies fail.
The ventral posteriomedial (VPM) nucleus of the thalamus relays sensory information from the face.
Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) deficiency causes hyperphenylalaninemia and neurotransmitter deficiency — requires more than dietary treatment.
The paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) generates horizontal saccades — lesions cause ipsilateral gaze palsy.
Spinal dysraphism encompasses a spectrum from spina bifida occulta to myelomeningocele.
Dandy-Walker malformation features cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, cerebellar vermis hypoplasia, and enlarged posterior fossa.
Hemiplegic migraine can be sporadic or familial (CACNA1A, ATP1A2, SCN1A) and mimics acute stroke.
The tectal plate (quadrigeminal plate) comprises the superior and inferior colliculi and is a common site for pediatric gliomas.
Becker muscular dystrophy is allelic to Duchenne (both dystrophin gene) but has later onset and milder course due to partially functional dystrophin.
The anterior nucleus of the thalamus is part of the Papez circuit and receives input from the mammillothalamic tract.
Nonaccidental trauma (abusive head trauma) is the leading cause of traumatic death in infants — retinal hemorrhages are a key (transient) finding.
Glutaric aciduria type 2 (multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency) can present with a 'sweaty feet' odor.
The vein of Galen malformation is actually a malformation of the median prosencephalic vein of Markowski.
Migraine is the most common cause of recurrent headache in children, affecting up to 8-10% of school-age children.
SLC6A1 mutations cause myoclonic-atonic epilepsy and are one of the most common genetic epilepsies.
The periaqueductal gray (PAG) surrounds the cerebral aqueduct and plays a key role in pain modulation and autonomic function.
Intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) in children can be caused by vitamin A excess, tetracycline, or growth hormone therapy.
Zonisamide has multiple mechanisms including sodium channel blockade, T-type calcium channel inhibition, and carbonic anhydrase inhibition.
Spinal cord infarction in children is rare but can follow aortic surgery, thrombophilia, or fibrocartilaginous embolism.
Childhood-onset schizophrenia (before age 13) is rare and must be distinguished from autoimmune encephalitis.
The superior orbital fissure transmits CN III, IV, V1, and VI between the middle cranial fossa and the orbit.
Hyperekplexia (startle disease) is caused by mutations in glycine receptor genes (GLRA1) and responds to clonazepam.
Hypomelanosis of Ito is a neurocutaneous disorder featuring whorled hypopigmentation along Blaschko's lines with CNS involvement.
The medullary raphe nuclei are the primary source of serotonin to the spinal cord.
Progressive supranuclear palsy features vertical gaze palsy — the initial deficit is in downgaze saccades.
Acute transverse myelitis in children requires urgent MRI and consideration of MOG antibody-associated disease.
The incidence of epilepsy is highest in the first year of life compared to any other age.
Generalized epilepsy with febrile seizures plus (GEFS+) is associated with SCN1A and SCN1B mutations.
The central tegmental tract connects the red nucleus to the inferior olive and is often hyperintense on MRI in metabolic disorders.
The amygdala is composed of ~13 nuclei and plays a central role in fear conditioning and emotional memory.
Multifocal motor neuropathy with conduction block is associated with anti-GM1 antibodies and responds to IVIG.
The caudate nucleus head bulges into the lateral wall of the frontal horn of the lateral ventricle.
Epileptic spasms can occur outside of infancy and are then classified under the broader term 'epileptic spasms syndrome.'
The stria medullaris thalami connects the septal area and habenula, forming part of the limbic-midbrain axis.
Lamotrigine requires slow titration to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome, especially when co-administered with valproic acid.
Perisylvian polymicrogyria can cause oromotor dysfunction, epilepsy, and mild intellectual disability.
The massa intermedia (interthalamic adhesion) connects the two thalami across the third ventricle and is absent in ~20% of people.
Oxcarbazepine can cause clinically significant hyponatremia, more commonly than carbamazepine.
Neonatal seizures are most commonly caused by hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, accounting for ~40-60% of cases.
Sturge-Weber syndrome affects only the ipsilateral hemisphere — the port-wine stain follows the V1 dermatome.
The nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) is essential for the baroreceptor reflex arc.
Selective dorsal rhizotomy can reduce spasticity in children with diplegic cerebral palsy by sectioning dorsal rootlets.
Epilepsy affects approximately 0.5-1% of children worldwide, making it one of the most common neurological disorders of childhood.
The Dejerine-Sottas syndrome (HMSN type III) is a severe demyelinating neuropathy presenting in infancy with delayed motor milestones.
Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) is an AAV9-based gene replacement therapy for SMA that delivers a functional SMN1 gene in a single IV dose.
The ILAE 2022 classification expanded developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEEs) to over 30 distinct genetic entities.
Antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) therapy tofersen targets SOD1 in ALS and represents a paradigm shift in treating neurodegenerative diseases with gene-specific therapy.
CRISPR-based in vivo gene editing has entered clinical trials for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, targeting exon skipping to restore dystrophin reading frame.
MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) is now recognized as a distinct entity from MS and NMOSD, with its own diagnostic criteria published in 2023.
The KCNQ2 gene is now recognized as one of the most common causes of neonatal-onset developmental and epileptic encephalopathy.
Cenobamate, approved for focal epilepsy, has a unique dual mechanism — it enhances GABA-A receptor currents and inhibits persistent sodium currents.
Ganaxolone, a neurosteroid modulator of GABA-A receptors, was FDA-approved in 2022 specifically for seizures in CDKL5 deficiency disorder.
The EPISTOP trial demonstrated that preventive antiseizure treatment in TSC infants (before seizure onset) improved developmental outcomes.
The glymphatic system — a brain-wide perivascular clearance pathway most active during sleep — was only discovered in 2012 and has transformed understanding of neurodegeneration.
Stereo-EEG (SEEG) has largely replaced subdural grid monitoring for presurgical epilepsy evaluation in many pediatric centers due to lower morbidity.
Fenfluramine, originally a weight-loss drug, was repurposed and FDA-approved for Dravet syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome seizures.
Soticlestat, a cholesterol 24-hydroxylase inhibitor, represents a novel mechanism of antiseizure action by modulating brain cholesterol metabolism.
MRI-guided laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) is increasingly used for minimally invasive ablation of epileptogenic foci in children, including hypothalamic hamartomas.
Newborn screening for SMA is now universal across all 50 US states, enabling presymptomatic treatment that dramatically improves outcomes.
Intrathecal enzyme replacement therapy (cerliponase alfa) for CLN2 Batten disease was the first FDA-approved treatment for any form of NCL.
Risdiplam (Evrysdi) is the first oral SMN2 splicing modifier for SMA, offering an alternative to intrathecal nusinersen and gene therapy.
The concept of 'developmental encephalopathy' was introduced to distinguish epilepsies where developmental impairment results from the genetic cause itself, not just seizures.
Trofinetide (Daybue) was FDA-approved in 2023 as the first treatment specifically for Rett syndrome, targeting IGF-1 signaling.
STXBP1 encephalopathy is now recognized as one of the most common genetic causes of early-onset epileptic encephalopathy after SCN1A.
The ketogenic diet has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects beyond seizure control, including in traumatic brain injury models.
Aquaporin-4 antibodies (AQP4-IgG) in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder can now be detected with cell-based assays with >99% specificity.
Eladocagene exuparvovec is an AAV2-based gene therapy for AADC deficiency — one of the first gene therapies for a pediatric neurotransmitter disorder.
CAR-T cell therapy is being explored for autoimmune neurological diseases like refractory anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and myasthenia gravis.
Newborn screening for X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy is now performed in most US states, enabling early identification before neurological decline.
The ILAE now recognizes 'etiology-specific' epilepsy syndromes where the genetic cause defines the syndrome (e.g., SCN1A-related, KCNQ2-related).
The NORSE/FIRES Registry has identified anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) as a promising immunotherapy for super-refractory status epilepticus.
Single-gene testing is being replaced by comprehensive epilepsy gene panels (500+ genes) and whole exome/genome sequencing as first-line genetic workup.
The 'two-hit' hypothesis in epilepsy suggests that a germline genetic variant plus a somatic 'second hit' in brain tissue may explain focal cortical dysplasia.
Glial cells, once thought to be passive support cells, are now known to actively modulate synaptic transmission and are implicated in epileptogenesis.
Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects mediated through the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway.
Neonatal EEG monitoring has revealed that >80% of electrographic seizures in critically ill neonates are subclinical — without visible clinical correlates.
Wearable seizure detection devices using accelerometry, EMG, or electrodermal activity are approaching clinical-grade accuracy for convulsive seizures.
Brexanolone (IV allopregnanolone) and its mechanism of GABA-A neurosteroid modulation has opened a new therapeutic class being explored for super-refractory SE in children.
The discovery that somatic mTOR pathway mutations cause type II focal cortical dysplasia has led to trials of mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) as targeted antiseizure therapy.
Optogenetic techniques in animal models can now selectively silence seizure-generating neurons while preserving normal circuit function.
Long-read DNA sequencing technologies (PacBio, Oxford Nanopore) are resolving previously undetectable structural variants and repeat expansions in unsolved genetic epilepsies.
Padsevonil, a dual SV2A/GABA-A modulator, represents a first-in-class compound designed specifically for drug-resistant epilepsy using structure-based rational drug design.
The ESES/CSWS pattern (continuous spike-wave during slow-wave sleep) must occupy >85% of non-REM sleep to meet diagnostic criteria and is associated with cognitive regression.
Doose syndrome (myoclonic-atonic epilepsy) has a 60-80% seizure-freedom rate with valproic acid or the ketogenic diet, but carbamazepine and vigabatrin can worsen seizures.
The TAND checklist (TSC-Associated Neuropsychiatric Disorders) was developed to screen for the behavioral, psychiatric, and cognitive comorbidities seen in up to 90% of TSC patients.
Neonatal arterial ischemic stroke most commonly involves the left middle cerebral artery and presents with focal seizures in the first 72 hours of life.
The SCN8A gene encodes Nav1.6 — gain-of-function mutations cause severe early-onset DEE, while loss-of-function mutations cause intellectual disability without epilepsy.
Everolimus (an mTOR inhibitor) is FDA-approved for TSC-associated subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs) and has antiseizure effects in TSC-related epilepsy.
The critical period for binocular vision development extends to approximately age 7-8 years — amblyopia treatment is most effective before this window closes.
Ataluren was the first drug designed to enable ribosomal read-through of premature stop codons, initially developed for nonsense-mutation Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
Anti-LGI1 encephalitis characteristically causes faciobrachial dystonic seizures — brief, frequent, unilateral contractions of the face and arm that often precede cognitive decline.
The International FIRES Consortium found that immunotherapy within 72 hours of refractory status epilepticus onset in FIRES was associated with better cognitive outcomes.
Cerebral creatine deficiency (GAMT deficiency) is one of the few treatable intellectual disabilities — creatine supplementation can prevent neurological decline if started early.
Genetic testing now identifies a cause in approximately 40-50% of early-onset epileptic encephalopathies, compared to <10% a decade ago.
The NORSE Institute has established that new-onset refractory status epilepticus has a cryptogenic cause in ~50% of cases, with autoimmune etiology being the most common identifiable cause.

Intravenous valproic acid can cause hyperammonemia even at therapeutic levels, particularly in patients with underlying urea cycle defects — ammonia should be checked in unexplained encephalopathy on VPA.
The SUDEP (Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy) rate in Dravet syndrome is estimated at 15-20% by adulthood, one of the highest of any epilepsy syndrome.
Omaveloxolone (Skyclarys) was FDA-approved in 2023 as the first treatment for Friedreich ataxia, working by activating the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway.

The concept of "precision ASM therapy" is becoming reality — for example, quinidine for KCNT1-related epilepsy and memantine for GRIN2A-related disorders target the specific dysfunctional ion channel.
